web: Vertical orb web (often rebuilt daily)
habitat: Gardens, hedgerows, woodland edges, near outdoor lights
region: Common and widespread across mainland Portugal
desc: One of the most recognisable garden spiders. A white cross of spots marks the abdomen, whose base colour ranges from pale yellow to dark grey-brown. It usually hangs head-down at the centre of its web.

medical.text: Not medically significant. Reluctant to bite; any bite is trivial.

fact: Also called the European garden spider or cross spider.

web: Loose, irregular web in ceiling corners
habitat: Indoors — corners, cellars, ceilings, sheds
region: Abundant inside Portuguese homes
desc: The classic "daddy long-legs" of the house corner: a tiny body slung beneath extraordinarily long, thread-thin legs. When disturbed it whirls itself in the web into a blur.

medical.text: Not medically significant. A long-standing myth claims it is highly venomous — it is not.

fact: It leaves its own web to hunt and eat other spiders.

web: No snare web; builds a silk "nursery tent" for the young
habitat: Grassland, scrub, sunny low vegetation
region: Common in meadows and field margins
desc: A wandering hunter, often seen basking with its front legs held together. The female carries her large egg sac in her jaws, then spins a tent of silk and stands guard over the hatchlings.
fact: Courting males offer the female a silk-wrapped insect as a "nuptial gift" — and may play dead to escape afterwards.

web: Flat sheet web with a funnel-shaped retreat
habitat: Houses, sheds, garages, wall crevices
region: Common around and inside buildings
desc: The big, fast, leggy spider that turns up in baths and along skirting boards, especially in autumn when wandering males are on the move. Despite the startle factor, it is a harmless insect-hunter.

medical.text: Not medically significant. It runs from people rather than toward them.

fact: It is found in bathtubs because it falls in chasing prey and cannot climb the smooth sides back out.

web: None — ambushes prey on flowers
habitat: Flowers in meadows, especially yellow and red umbels
region: Widespread in sunny flowery habitats
desc: A rounded crab spider that lies in wait on blossoms with its long front legs spread like pincers. Females come in red, yellow or white forms, each with a dark abdominal mark.

medical.text: Not medically significant; fangs are too small to matter to humans.

fact: Its name comes from the abdominal mark, said to resemble the silhouette of Napoleon in his hat.

web: Orb web
habitat: Shrubs, hedgerows, coastal scrub, woodland
region: Common, often along the coast
desc: A small orb-weaver with a pair of low humps near the front of a usually greenish abdomen — good camouflage among leaves where it rests during the day.
fact: The greenish tone helps it disappear against the foliage it shelters in.

web: Orb web with a zig-zag silk band (stabilimentum)
habitat: Tall grass and low vegetation in warm, sunny spots
region: Locally common in grassy habitats
desc: Unmistakable: the female wears bold yellow, black and white bands like a wasp. Her web carries a distinctive vertical zig-zag of dense silk through the middle.

medical.text: Not medically significant. A bite is rare and at most like a mild sting.

fact: The wasp-like colours are thought to warn off birds, even though the spider is harmless.

web: Horizontal sheet web with a funnel and maze-like retreat
habitat: Grassland, scrub and low bushes
region: Common in open grassy places
desc: It spins a broad sheet of silk over low vegetation, with a tubular retreat leading into a "labyrinth" of tunnels behind. The spider waits at the funnel mouth and rushes out when prey hits the sheet.
fact: The deep, branching retreat that gives the species its name is also where the female guards her egg sacs.

web: None — uses silk safety lines while stalking
habitat: Sunny walls, tree trunks and buildings
region: Very common on Mediterranean walls
desc: A flattened, greyish jumping spider that patrols sunlit walls, turning its big forward eyes to track prey before pouncing. Tame and inquisitive at close range.
fact: Jumping spiders have some of the sharpest vision of any animal their size.

web: None — ambushes prey on flowers
habitat: Heather and flowering scrub in sunny areas
region: Frequent in flowery scrub, especially in the south
desc: An angular crab spider with raised "shoulders" on the abdomen. Females may be pink, white or yellow and can slowly shift colour to match the flower they ambush from.
fact: Colour change to match a flower can take a few days, not seconds.

web: Large orb web
habitat: Dry, sunny Mediterranean scrub
region: More common in the warm south
desc: A big, striking orb-weaver with a silvery abdomen edged in scalloped lobes, giving a crinkled, leaf-like outline. It sits openly at the hub of a large web in hot, dry places.
fact: The scalloped abdomen and silvery sheen make it look almost metallic in sunlight.

web: None — an active hunter among leaves
habitat: Green vegetation, woodland edges and meadows
region: Widespread in leafy habitats
desc: A vivid grass-green spider that vanishes against foliage. It does not build a web but stalks prey across leaves; the male adds red and yellow stripes to his slimmer abdomen.
fact: It is one of the few truly bright-green spiders in Europe.

web: Orb web spun at night
habitat: Under bark, fences, walls and dead wood
region: Common but easily overlooked
desc: A very flat, dark spider built to slip into narrow crevices in bark or fence posts by day. At dusk it emerges to build an orb web nearby, retreating again before dawn.
fact: Its strongly flattened body lets it wedge into cracks barely wider than a coin.

web: None — captures prey by spitting
habitat: Indoors and sheltered walls; slow-moving
region: Widespread but quiet and nocturnal
desc: A pale, delicately patterned spider with a high domed front. It hunts by squirting a zig-zag of sticky, venom-laced gum over prey from a short distance, pinning it in place before approaching.
fact: It can spit its gluey trap from roughly a body-length away.

web: None
habitat: Warm rocky and grassy slopes in the sun
region: Locally common on sunny ground
desc: A bold jumping spider; the male has a brilliant red abdomen split by a black central band. Like all jumpers it tracks prey with large eyes and leaps to seize it.
fact: Males display their red colours and wave their legs in courtship "dances".

web: None — a free-ranging hunter
habitat: Woodland; increasingly inside houses in the Mediterranean
region: Mediterranean; sometimes enters homes
desc: Often mistaken for a wolf spider, but in a different family. It is a large, patterned night-hunter that has adapted to living indoors, where it pursues prey across walls and floors.

medical.text: Bites are rare and at most cause brief, localised pain — not medically dangerous.

fact: Its move indoors has let it spread well beyond its original range.

web: None — a ground hunter, sometimes from a burrow
habitat: Dry open ground, scrub and sunny slopes
region: Frequent in dry open country
desc: A large, robust wolf spider with a radiating pattern on the carapace. It chases prey across the ground rather than spinning a snare. The female carries her egg sac attached to her spinnerets, then the spiderlings ride on her back.

medical.text: A bite can be painful but is not medically dangerous.

fact: Old Mediterranean "tarantism" dances were often blamed on large wolf spiders like this.

web: Messy tangle (cobweb)
habitat: Walls, fences and around buildings
region: Established and spreading around towns
desc: A glossy, bulbous brown spider with a cream pattern on the abdomen that some see as a faint skull. Frequently confused with the true black widow, but far less serious.

medical.text: Not dangerous. A bite can be painful with local swelling, broadly like a wasp sting; it usually settles on its own.

fact: Despite alarming headlines, verified bites are uncommon and serious effects are rare.

size: Female up to 22 mm · male up to 15 mm
web: Tubular retreat with radiating trip-lines
habitat: Old walls and crevices, often near buildings
region: Common around old stone and brickwork
desc: A glossy black spider that lives head-out in a silk tube wedged into a wall crack, with signal threads fanning from the entrance. Touch a thread and it strikes from the tube.

medical.text: A bite can be painful but is not medically dangerous.

fact: Its fangs (chelicerae) shine an iridescent metallic green.

web: Irregular threads in undisturbed retreats
habitat: Dark, undisturbed places — behind furniture, storerooms, also urban indoors
region: Present in Portugal, including towns; rarely encountered in the open
desc: A small, plain tan spider with long legs and a darker violin-shaped mark on the front body. It is shy and stays hidden, biting only when pressed against the skin — for instance inside clothing or bedding.

medical.text: One of only two spiders of recognised medical significance in Portugal. Most bites are minor, but a bite can occasionally cause a slow-healing skin lesion (loxoscelism). Many wounds blamed on recluses are actually something else. If you are bitten and feel unwell, seek medical advice.

fact: It has six eyes in three pairs — unusual among common spiders, which mostly have eight.

web: Strong, irregular tangle near the ground
habitat: Dry, sunny grassland, steppe and field margins (rarely inside homes)
region: Local in dry open country, not typically in houses
desc: A rounded, satin-black widow spider whose abdomen carries up to thirteen red, orange or yellow spots. Shy and reclusive, it stays low in dense vegetation. Only the much larger female can deliver a meaningful bite.

medical.text: One of only two spiders of recognised medical significance in Portugal. The female’s venom can cause latrodectism — pain, sweating and muscle cramps. It is rarely life-threatening in healthy adults, but medical attention is advised if bitten. The spider is shy and bites are uncommon.

fact: Its Latin name means "thirteen-spotted"; in some regions it is called the malmignatte.

size: Body to ~30 mm+ — the largest spider in Europe
web: Funnel/sheet web among stones, roots and leaf litter
habitat: Cork-oak woodland and scrub, mainly in the south
region: Local and patchy in Portugal, chiefly the Algarve
desc: A heavy, satin-black mygalomorph (a relative of tarantulas) with strikingly long spinnerets. Europe’s largest spider, it lives in a funnel of silk among rocks and roots and is rarely seen.

medical.text: Imposing, but its venom is not dangerous to humans (a bite is painful). It is protected — do not collect or disturb it.

fact: It is the only spider species protected under European Union legislation.

size: ~0.4–0.5 mm — among the smallest spiders known
web: Minute web
habitat: A few caves in southern Portugal
region: Endemic to Portugal and extremely localised
desc: A true micro-spider, smaller than a pinhead, known only from caves in the Algarve. It is a Portuguese endemic of high conservation concern and is almost never seen — a record-keeper’s holy grail more than a field encounter.

medical.text: Completely harmless — far too small to bite a human.

fact: It is named after Ataegina, an underworld goddess of the ancient Lusitanians.

web: None
habitat: Sunny walls, rocks, fences and buildings
region: Common on sunlit walls everywhere
desc: A small, charismatic jumper boldly marked in black and white stripes. It stalks prey in short bursts, swivelling its big front eyes to keep target in view, then jumps to seize it — tethered by a silk safety line.
fact: It often turns to "look at" a watching human, one of the most engaging spiders to meet.

web: None — ambushes from flowers
habitat: Flower heads in meadows, gardens and verges
diet: Bees, hoverflies and other flower visitors
region: Common across mainland Portugal in flowery places
desc: A squat, crab-shaped ambusher that waits inside a flower with its long front legs spread. Over several days it can slowly shift between white and yellow to match the bloom. It regularly takes prey far larger than itself.

medical.text: Not medically significant. The fangs rarely pierce human skin.

fact: The colour change is one-sided in speed: turning yellow takes days, while turning back to white is quicker.

web: None — rests in a silk sac under cover
habitat: Under stones, logs, bark and pots; sometimes indoors at floor level
region: Common and widespread across Portugal
desc: A ground hunter with a glossy reddish front body, a pale cream abdomen and strikingly large, forward-pointing fangs. Those fangs are built to pierce the armour of woodlice, prey that few other predators will tackle.

medical.text: Not dangerous, but the large fangs can give a brief, sharp nip if the spider is carelessly handled. Any effect is short-lived.

fact: It is one of very few predators that specialise in eating woodlice.

web: Loose tangle web in sheltered nooks
habitat: Porches, sheds, walls and vegetation, often outdoors
region: Common in the south and centre, spreading north
desc: A long-legged cellar spider like the familiar indoor one, but with a more marbled, oval body and a habit of using sheltered spots outdoors as well as inside. Several individuals may share one loose communal web.
fact: Like other cellar spiders, it whirls its body into a blur when the web is disturbed.

web: Orb web with one open sector and a signal line
habitat: Window frames, doorways, fences and walls near buildings
region: Common around buildings throughout Portugal
desc: A small orb-weaver known for the gap in its web: it leaves one sector unspun, with a single signal thread running up to a silk retreat in a corner. It waits there with one leg on the line, feeling for prey.
fact: The open sector is no mistake — the signal line through it gives the spider the fastest route to struggling prey.

web: Small orb web among leaves
habitat: Shrubs, hedgerows and woodland edges
region: Common in green places across Portugal
desc: A tiny, bright apple-green orb-weaver with a rounded abdomen, usually found at a small web on the underside of a leaf. The vivid green is only seen in life, as it fades after death.
fact: Despite the vivid green back, there is a small red mark underneath, near the tip of the abdomen.

web: Delicate, finely meshed orb web low in vegetation
habitat: Grassland, heath and sunny field margins
region: Common in open grassy habitats
desc: A small, pale orb-weaver of grassland whose abdomen carries a dark central mark that some read as the shape of a cricket bat. Its web is unusually fine and densely meshed for the spider size.
fact: Its web has more spokes and a finer mesh than most orb-weavers of similar size.

web: Open, near-horizontal orb web by water
habitat: Reeds and tall grass beside ponds, ditches and rivers
region: Common beside fresh water across Portugal
desc: A slender, straw-coloured orb-weaver with a very long body and long jaws. When resting on a stem it stretches its legs fore and aft and lies flat along it, becoming very hard to spot.
fact: During mating the long jaws of the two spiders interlock, holding each other safely apart.

web: Vertical orb web near water and on structures
habitat: Damp vegetation, reeds, bridges and fences near water
region: Common near water and on structures throughout Portugal
desc: A sturdy, usually nocturnal orb-weaver with a pale-edged dark mark down the abdomen, often likened to an oak leaf or a furrow. By day it hides in a rolled leaf or crevice beside the web.
fact: It is one of the orb-weavers most often found on bridges and waterside railings.

web: None — an active, sighted hunter on plants
habitat: Sunny low vegetation, scrub and grassland
diet: Insects caught by stalking and pouncing
region: Frequent in warm, sunny habitats, especially in the south
desc: A pale, alert hunter with spiny legs and six of its eyes set in a hexagon for sharp forward vision. It stalks and pounces on prey among plants rather than building a web, often in short, lynx-like leaps.
fact: Lynx spiders rely on keen eyesight and quick pounces, hunting more like jumping spiders than typical web-builders.

web: Dense, domed horizontal sheet, often in colonies
habitat: Bushes, cactus, fences and groves in warm areas
region: Established in the south, notably the Algarve, and spreading
desc: An orb-weaver that builds not a flat orb but a finely meshed, horizontal dome of silk, often in loose colonies where many webs join. It sits beneath the dome and is a striking sight on warm-country fences and shrubs.
fact: Many individuals can web together into large connected colonies, unusual among orb-weavers.

web: None — builds a silk sac retreat
habitat: Tall grassland and scrub; the larger species may enter buildings
region: Present in warm grassy habitats; far less often met than the harmless spiders above
desc: A pale straw to greenish hunter with dark jaw tips that builds a silk sac to shelter in by day and hunts by night. This is the European spider most often singled out for a noticeably sharp bite, though serious effects are rare.

medical.text: Among the few European spiders that can deliver a bite felt like a bee or wasp sting, with local pain and swelling that usually settles within a day or two. Not considered dangerous, and severe reactions are uncommon. Seek medical advice if you feel unwell.

fact: It is one of the few spiders in Europe whose bite is regularly noticed by people — yet the effect is usually no worse than a wasp sting.

web: Horizontal orb web of woolly (cribellate) silk
habitat: Sheltered foliage, garden centres and greenhouses
region: Established around the country, often in greenhouses and indoors
desc: A small, twig-like orb-weaver that holds its body flat with tufts of hair on its front legs. Remarkably, it belongs to the one orb-weaver family that has lost its venom glands entirely, and so wraps prey in vast amounts of silk rather than biting it.

medical.text: Not medically significant — this spider has no venom glands at all.

fact: It is one of the very few spiders on Earth with no venom glands, subduing prey by silk-wrapping alone.

web: Orb web in dark, damp spaces
habitat: Cave mouths, mines, cellars, wells and culverts
diet: Cave insects, woodlice, millipedes and stray moths
region: Local across Portugal wherever cool, dark, damp spaces occur
desc: A long-legged orb-weaver of the twilight zone of caves and cellars that shuns daylight. Adults hang in the dark, and the spherical egg sacs dangle from the ceiling on long threads. It is among the most reliably cave-dwelling spiders of Europe.
fact: Its hatchlings are drawn toward light, which helps them leave the cave to disperse before later seeking the dark again.

size: Female to about 30 mm · leg span near 70 mm
web: None — lives in a silk-lined burrow
habitat: Dry, open ground, scrub and field margins in warm areas
diet: Large insects ambushed from the burrow mouth
region: Local in warm, dry parts of Portugal; much larger than the common wolf spider
desc: A large ground wolf spider that lives in a vertical burrow and rushes out to seize passing prey. Despite its size and old reputation, it is reluctant to bite, and its venom is geared to subduing insects rather than harming people.

medical.text: A defensive bite is possible if the spider is cornered or handled, and can be painful — roughly comparable to a bee sting — but it is not considered dangerous. Seek medical advice if you feel unwell.

fact: This spider gave its name to both the word tarantula and the tarantella, a dance once believed to cure its bite.

size: Female to about 15 mm · male to about 12 mm
web: Tangle of catching threads spread from a silk tube
habitat: Dry, sunny scrub and open ground in the Mediterranean zone
region: A Mediterranean species of warm, dry country; the only European Stegodyphus
desc: A velvety, pale-and-dark spider of hot, dry scrub that spins a silk retreat with a spread of catching threads. It is best known for an extraordinary act of motherhood at the end of its life.
fact: After feeding her young on regurgitated food, the mother lets them consume her body — a final meal that gives them a strong start. This is called matriphagy.

web: Silk-lined burrow with a sheet of catching silk at ground level
habitat: Warm, sunny slopes with sparse vegetation and bare ground
region: Local in warm, dry habitats of the Iberian Peninsula
desc: A velvet spider whose adult male is one of the most spectacular in Europe: bright red with bold black spots, like a walking ladybird. Females are velvety black and spend their lives in a burrow, rarely seen above ground.
fact: Only the small male wears the famous red-and-black colours, and only as an adult, when he leaves the burrow to wander in search of a female.

web: None — shelters in a small dome of silk and sand
habitat: Bare, stony ground in warm, open country
region: An endemic of mainland Portugal, named for the Alentejo
desc: A tiny, ant-like spider that lives among ants and hunts them, walking with a jerky, ant-mimicking gait. It shelters under a small igloo of silk studded with sand grains. This species is found only in Portugal.
fact: Ant-eating spiders both resemble and prey upon ants, moving unnoticed among the colonies that feed them.

web: Strong tangle web low among stones and plants
habitat: Open, dry steppe and scrub, away from buildings
region: A widow of the dry Iberian interior, in Portugal and Spain
desc: A close relative of the Mediterranean black widow that lives out on open Iberian steppe and scrub rather than near people. It carries small pale and orange marks near the front of the abdomen and, like its relatives, builds a strong, untidy tangle web close to the ground.

medical.text: A true widow, but one of remote open country rather than homes, so encounters are unlikely. As a precaution its bite is best treated like that of other widows; the effects of its venom are not well documented. Seek medical advice if bitten and feeling unwell.

fact: Unlike its American cousins that often live close to people, this widow keeps to remote open steppe.

web: Small flat star-shaped silk patch flush to a surface
habitat: Walls, around windows and skirting, indoors and out
region: Common but easily overlooked on walls throughout Portugal
desc: A minute, flattened spider that lives under a small star-shaped patch of silk pressed against a wall. When an ant or other small insect passes, it darts out in a quick circle, laying silk around the prey before it can escape.
fact: For its size it is astonishingly fast, circling prey in a fraction of a second to lay down restraining silk.

web: Bluish woolly (cribellate) lace silk around a crevice
habitat: Cracks in walls, under stones, logs and bark, often near buildings
region: Widespread in shaded, damp places across Portugal
desc: A dark, robust spider that lines a crevice with bluish, woolly lace-like silk and waits at the entrance. The combed silk has a faintly electric look when fresh. Its family is known for a remarkable feeding arrangement between mother and young.
fact: The mother lays a batch of special unfertilised eggs purely as food for her spiderlings, before they finally consume her as well.

size: Female 9–14 mm · male smaller with longer legs
web: Woolly (cribellate) silk lines spreading from a crevice
habitat: Cracks in old walls, rocks and bark in warm, dry places
region: Common in old walls and stonework in the south and centre
desc: A long-lived spider of warm walls and rockwork that lives deep in a crevice, with a fan of woolly silk lines radiating out across the surface. Its eyes are clustered together on a small central mound, and females may live for several years.
fact: Females are long-lived and tend the same crevice and web for years, enlarging it over time.
